insights: Resolution times increased 15-fold from 141 to 2097 hours between January and October 2023, reflecting a systemic degradation in incident closure capability rather than isolated performance issues.

The trend is uniform across all assignment groups, priority levels, categories, and assignees — with no statistically significant differences observed (all ANOVA p > 0.20) and only ~4% of variance explained collectively — indicating the driver lies outside current operational dimensions.

Coefficient of variation in resolution time collapsed from 0.437 to 0.034 over the period, confirming the trend is driven by consistent, pervasive pressure rather than outlier incidents or increasing heterogeneity.

Incident composition remained stable (e.g., Network consistently 50–70%, Priority 2-High 60–85%), ruling out shifts in incident type or severity as an explanation for the 15x resolution time increase.

The baseline resolution time of 1043 hours (~43 days) for resolved incidents — coupled with 25.6% unresolved incidents (missing closed_at) — signals chronic service capacity strain that intensified progressively over time.
With R² = 0.9996 and slope = +218.47 hours/month, the linear escalation is both statistically robust and operationally alarming: every month added nearly 9 additional days to average resolution time.
summary: The analysis confirms a severe, systematic deterioration in incident resolution capability: resolution times escalated 15-fold — from 141 to 2097 hours — over 10 months, with near-perfect linearity (R²=0.9996) and uniformity across all available operational dimensions (assignment group, priority, category, assignee). This pattern cannot be attributed to changes in incident mix, team composition, or priority distribution, nor to outliers or random variation — as evidenced by collapsing coefficient of variation and negligible explanatory power (<4%) of all categorical drivers. The high baseline resolution time (~43 days), combined with 25.6% unresolved incidents, points to sustained resource or process constraints at the organizational level. Given the absence of relevant fields (e.g., headcount, SLA policy changes, backlog volume, external dependencies) in the dataset, the next critical step is to investigate systemic enablers — particularly staffing levels, policy revisions, tooling limitations, or upstream dependency delays — through operational interviews and supplemental data collection.
